name: Izumo
description: Izumo, located in present-day Shimane Prefecture, holds immense significance in Japanese mythology and Shinto religion. It is often regarded as the land of the gods, a place where deities gather and where many foundational myths of Japan unfolded. The Grand Shrine of Izumo (Izumo Taisha) is one of the oldest and most important shrines in Japan, dedicated to Ōkuninushi, the god of nation-building, agriculture, and medicine. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the land of Izumo was originally ruled by Ōkuninushi, who later ceded it to Ninigi-no-Mikoto, the grandson of Amaterasu, the sun goddess, solidifying the divine lineage of the imperial family. This event is a cornerstone of Japanese mythology, representing the transfer of power from the earthly deities (kunitsukami) to the heavenly deities (amatsukami). Izumo is also associated with numerous other myths, including the story of Susanoo, the storm god, slaying the Yamata no Orochi, an eight-headed serpent, and subsequently finding the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword within its tail. The region is believed to be a spiritual power spot, attracting pilgrims and visitors seeking blessings and connection to the ancient gods. The annual Kamiari-sai festival, held in the tenth month of the lunar calendar (Kannazuki – the month without gods elsewhere), sees deities from across Japan gather at Izumo Taisha for their annual meeting, further reinforcing Izumo's central role in the divine realm. The architectural style of Izumo Taisha is unique and ancient, reflecting the long history and sacredness of the site.
subtitle: Land of the Gods and Ancient Legends

keyMyths.title: Ōkuninushi Cedes the Land
keyMyths.description: Ōkuninushi relinquishes control of Izumo to Ninigi-no-Mikoto, grandson of Amaterasu, establishing the divine right of the imperial family. This event marks a transition from earthly to heavenly deities.

keyMyths.title: Susanoo and the Yamata no Orochi
keyMyths.description: Susanoo, banished from the heavens, slays the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi in Izumo, retrieving the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword. This act demonstrates Susanoo's heroic nature and connects Izumo to a powerful artifact.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle in Japan.
sources.text: English translation of the Kojiki, the oldest extant chronicle in Japan.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.description: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important chronicle of Japanese history and mythology.
